content:
# Leviticus Chapter 8 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 61v–62r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_008_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_008_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 92–93 | `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/LEV_008_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_008_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

*[Note on structure: Leviticus 8 narrates the ordination (millu'im, lit. "filling") ceremony for Aharon and his sons, performed by Moshe as the LORD's designated officiant — the only extended passage in the Pentateuch in which Moshe functions as priest. The chapter divides structurally: (1) vv.1-5: divine command and assembly; (2) vv.6-9: washing and vesting with all the priestly garments; (3) vv.10-13: anointing the tabernacle, its vessels, the altar, and Aharon; (4) vv.14-17: sin offering bull; (5) vv.18-21: burnt offering ram; (6) vv.22-29: ram of ordination (eil ha-millu'im), including the distinctive blood-on-ear-thumb-toe ritual; (7) v.30: sprinkling of oil and blood on Aharon and his sons; (8) vv.31-36: seven-day ordination period and Moshe's charge. MT and English versification are identical throughout this chapter.]*

---

**1** And the LORD spoke [vayedabber YHWH] to Moshe [el-Moshe], saying:

**2** "Take Aharon [qach et-Aharon] and his sons [ve-et-banav] with him, and the garments [ve-et-ha-begadim] and the anointing oil [ve-et shemen ha-mishchah] [the anointing oil — used to consecrate the tabernacle, its vessels, and the priests], and the bull of the sin offering [ve-et par ha-chattat] and the two rams [ve-et shney ha-eilim] and the basket of unleavened bread [ve-et sal ha-matsot],

**3** and assemble [ve-et kol-ha-edah haqhel] the whole congregation [kol-ha-edah] to the entrance of the tent of meeting [el-petach ohel mo'ed]."

**4** And Moshe did [vaya'as Moshe] as the LORD commanded him [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH oto], and the congregation was assembled [vatiqahel ha-edah] at the entrance of the tent of meeting [el-petach ohel mo'ed].

**5** And Moshe said [vayomer Moshe] to the congregation [el-ha-edah]: "This is the thing [zeh ha-davar] that the LORD has commanded [asher-tsivvah YHWH] to do [la'asot]."

---

**6** And Moshe brought near [vayaqrev Moshe] Aharon and his sons [et-Aharon ve-et-banav] and he washed them [vayrachtsets otam] with water [ba-mayim].

**7** And he put upon him [vayittten alav] the tunic [et-ha-kuttonet] and girded him [vayachgor oto] with the sash [ba-avnet] and clothed him [vayilbash oto] with the robe [et-ha-me'il] and set upon him [vayittten alav] the ephod [et-ha-ephod] [the ephod — the ornate priestly vest worn over the robe] and girded him [vayachgor oto] with the decorated band of the ephod [be-cheshev ha-ephod] and bound it to him with it [vaye'ephod lo bo].

**8** And he placed upon him [vayasem alav] the breastpiece [et-ha-choshen] and put [vayittten] into the breastpiece [el-ha-choshen] the Urim and the Tummim [et-ha-urim ve-et-ha-tummim] [Urim ve-Tummim — sacred lots or oracular objects placed in the breastpiece; used for inquiring of the LORD; exact nature uncertain].

**9** And he set upon his head [vayasem al-rosho] the turban [et-ha-mitsnefet] and set upon the turban [al-ha-mitsnefet] at the front of it [el-mul panav] the golden plate [tsits ha-zahav] [tsits — the golden rosette/plate inscribed "Holy to the LORD"], the holy diadem [nezer ha-qodesh] — as the LORD commanded Moshe [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe].

---

**10** And Moshe took [vayiqach Moshe] the anointing oil [et shemen ha-mishchah] and anointed [vayimshach] the tabernacle [et-ha-mishkan] and all that was in it [ve-et-kol-asher-bo] and consecrated them [vaiqaddesh otam].

**11** And he sprinkled from it [vayaz mimmennu] upon the altar [al-ha-mizbeach] seven times [sheva pe'amim] and anointed the altar [vayimshach et-ha-mizbeach] and all its vessels [ve-et-kol-kelav] and the basin [ve-et-ha-kiyyor] and its stand [ve-et-kano] to consecrate them [le-qaddesh otam].

**12** And he poured [vayitssoq] from the anointing oil [mi-shemen ha-mishchah] upon the head of Aharon [al-rosh Aharon] and anointed him [vayimshach oto] to consecrate him [le-qaddesh oto].

**13** And Moshe brought near [vayaqrev Moshe] the sons of Aharon [et-bney Aharon] and clothed them [vayalbesh otam] with tunics [kuttanot] and girded them [vayachgor otam] with sashes [avnet] and bound turbans on them [vayachbosh lahem migba'ot] — as the LORD commanded Moshe [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe].

---

**14** And he brought near [vayaqrev] the bull of the sin offering [et-par ha-chattat], and Aharon and his sons [vayismokh Aharon u-vanav] laid their hands [et-yedehem] upon the head of the bull of the sin offering [al-rosh par ha-chattat].

**15** And he slaughtered it [vayishchat] and Moshe took [vayiqach Moshe] the blood [et-ha-dam] and put [vayittten] on the horns of the altar [al-qarnot ha-mizbeach] all around [saviv] with his finger [be-etsba'o] and purified [vaychatte] the altar [et-ha-mizbeach] and the blood [ve-et-ha-dam] he poured [yatsaq] at the base of the altar [el-yesod ha-mizbeach] and consecrated it [vayeqaddesh oto] to make atonement over it [le-khapper alav].

**16** And he took [vayiqach] all the fat [et-kol-ha-chelev] that was on the entrails [asher al-ha-qerev] and the lobe of the liver [ve-et yoteret ha-kaved] and the two kidneys [ve-et shtey ha-kelayot] and their fat [ve-et-chelbahen] and Moshe burned [vayaqter Moshe] them on the altar [ha-mizbeachah].

**17** But the bull [ve-et ha-par] and its skin [ve-et-oro] and its flesh [ve-et-besaro] and its dung [ve-et-pir'sho] he burned with fire [saraf ba-esh] outside the camp [michutz la-machaneh] — as the LORD commanded Moshe [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe].

---

**18** And he brought near [vayaqrev] the ram of the burnt offering [et eil ha-olah], and Aharon and his sons [vayismokh Aharon u-vanav] laid their hands [et-yedehem] upon the head of the ram [al-rosh ha-ayil].

**19** And he slaughtered it [vayishchat] and Moshe dashed [vayizroq Moshe] the blood [et-ha-dam] against the altar [al-ha-mizbeach] on all sides [saviv].

**20** And the ram [ve-et ha-ayil] he cut [nitteach] into its pieces [linetochiav] and Moshe burned [vayaqter Moshe] the head [et-ha-rosh] and the pieces [ve-et-ha-netochiav] and the fat [ve-et-ha-pader].

**21** And the entrails [ve-et ha-qerev] and the legs [ve-et-ha-kera'ayim] he washed [rachats] with water [ba-mayim] and Moshe burned [vayaqter Moshe] the whole ram [et-kol-ha-ayil] on the altar [ha-mizbeachah] — it is a burnt offering [olah hu] for a pleasing aroma [le-re'ach nichoach] [re'ach nichoach — lit. "an aroma of rest/soothing"], a fire offering [isheh] to the LORD [la-YHWH] — as the LORD commanded Moshe [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe].

---

**22** And he brought near [vayaqrev] the second ram [et-ha-ayil ha-sheni], the ram of ordination [eil ha-millu'im] [eil ha-millu'im — "ram of the filling(s)"; millu'im derives from male' yad, "to fill the hand," an idiom for investiture into priestly office], and Aharon and his sons [vayismokh Aharon u-vanav] laid their hands [et-yedehem] upon the head of the ram [al-rosh ha-ayil].

**23** And he slaughtered it [vayishchat] and Moshe took [vayiqach Moshe] some of its blood [mi-damo] and put it [vayittten] on the lobe of the right ear of Aharon [al-tenukh ozen Aharon ha-yemanit] [tenukh ozen — the "lobe" or cartilaginous tip of the ear; the right ear, right thumb, and right big toe consecrate the whole person: what is heard, what is done, where one walks] and upon the thumb of his right hand [ve-al-bohen yado ha-yemanit] and upon the big toe of his right foot [ve-al-bohen raglo ha-yemanit].

**24** And the sons of Aharon were brought near [vayaqrev et-bney Aharon] and Moshe put [vayittten Moshe] some of the blood [min-ha-dam] upon the lobe of their right ear [al-tenukh oznam ha-yemanit] and upon the thumbs of their right hands [ve-al-bohen yedam ha-yemanit] and upon the big toes of their right feet [ve-al-bohen raglam ha-yemanit] and Moshe dashed [vayizroq Moshe] the blood [et-ha-dam] against the altar [al-ha-mizbeach] on all sides [saviv].

**25** And he took [vayiqach] the fat [et-ha-chelev] and the fat tail [ve-et-ha-alyah] and all the fat [ve-et-kol-ha-chelev] that was on the entrails [asher al-ha-qerev] and the lobe of the liver [ve-et yoteret ha-kaved] and the two kidneys [ve-et shtey ha-kelayot] and their fat [ve-et-chelbahen] and the right thigh [ve-et shok ha-yamin].

**26** And from the basket of unleavened bread [u-mi-sal ha-matsot] that was before the LORD [asher lifney YHWH] he took [laqach] one unleavened loaf [challat matsah achat] and one loaf of bread with oil [ve-challat lechem shemen achat] and one wafer [ve-rekik echad] and placed them [vayasem] on the fat portions [al-ha-chalavim] and on the right thigh [ve-al shok ha-yamin].

**27** And he placed all of it [vayasem et-kol] on the palms of Aharon [al-kapey Aharon] and on the palms of his sons [ve-al kapey vanav] and waved [vayannef] them as a wave offering [tenufah] before the LORD [lifney YHWH].

**28** And Moshe took [vayiqach Moshe] them from their palms [me-al kapeyhem] and burned them [vayaqter] on the altar [ha-mizbeachah] on top of the burnt offering [al-ha-olah] — they are an ordination offering [millu'im hem] for a pleasing aroma [le-re'ach nichoach], a fire offering [isheh] to the LORD [la-YHWH].

**29** And Moshe took [vayiqach Moshe] the breast [et-he-chazeh] and waved it [vayannifhu] as a wave offering [tenufah] before the LORD [lifney YHWH] — from the ram of ordination [me-eil ha-millu'im] it was Moshe's portion [hayah le-Moshe le-manah] — as the LORD commanded Moshe [ka'asher tsivvah YHWH et-Moshe].

---

**30** And Moshe took [vayiqach Moshe] from the anointing oil [mi-shemen ha-mishchah] and from the blood [u-min-ha-dam] that was on the altar [asher al-ha-mizbeach] and sprinkled [vayaz] on Aharon [al-Aharon] and on his garments [ve-al-begadav] and on his sons [ve-al-banav] and on the garments of his sons with him [ve-al-bigdey vanav ito] and he consecrated [vayeqaddesh] Aharon [et-Aharon] and his garments [ve-et-begadav] and his sons [ve-et-banav] and the garments of his sons with him [ve-et-bigdey vanav ito].

---

**31** And Moshe said [vayomer Moshe] to Aharon [el-Aharon] and to his sons [ve-el-banav]: "Boil the flesh [bashlu et-ha-basar] at the entrance of the tent of meeting [petach ohel mo'ed] and there [ve-sham] eat it [tochlu oto] and the bread [ve-et-ha-lechem] that is in the basket of ordination [asher be-sal ha-millu'im] — as I commanded [ka'asher tsivviti] saying [lemor]: 'Aharon and his sons shall eat it [Aharon u-vanav yokheluhu].'

**32** And what remains [ve-ha-notar] of the flesh [ba-basar] and of the bread [u-va-lechem] you shall burn with fire [ba-esh tisrofu].

**33** And from the entrance of the tent of meeting [u-mi-petach ohel mo'ed] you shall not go out [lo tetse'u] for seven days [shiv'at yamim] [shiv'at yamim — seven days; the ordination ceremony requires a full week's consecration; Moshe repeats the ceremony each day for seven days], until the day of the completion [ad yom melo'ot] of the days of your ordination [yemey millu'eikhem] — for seven days [ki shiv'at yamim] he shall ordain you [yemalle et-yedekhem] [yemalle et-yedekhem — lit. "he shall fill your hands," the technical idiom for priestly installation].

**34** As has been done [ka'asher na'asah] on this day [ba-yom ha-zeh], the LORD commanded [tsivvah YHWH] to do [la'asot] to make atonement for you [le-khapper aleykhem].

**35** And at the entrance of the tent of meeting [u-fetach ohel mo'ed] you shall remain [teshvu] day and night [yomam va-laylah] for seven days [shiv'at yamim] and you shall keep the charge of the LORD [u-shemartem et-mishmeret YHWH] that you may not die [ve-lo tamuttu] — for so I was commanded [ki-khen tsuvveiti].

**36** And Aharon and his sons [vaya'as Aharon u-vanav] did [et-kol-ha-devarim] all the things [asher tsivvah YHWH] that the LORD commanded [be-yad Moshe] through the hand of Moshe [kol-ha-devarim asher tsivvah YHWH be-yad Moshe].

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Leningrad Codex B19A, Folios 61v–62r)

1. **vv.1-5 — Structure: divine command and congregational assembly:** The opening of Lev 8 is marked by the standard revelatory formula וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה (vayedabber YHWH el-Moshe, "and the LORD spoke to Moses"), consistent with the introduction to each major legislative unit in Leviticus. What is structurally remarkable is that this instruction to Moshe — "take Aharon and his sons, the garments, the oil, the animals, the basket, and assemble the congregation" — is the fulfillment of the instructions already given in Ex 29 and the anointing formula of Ex 30:22-33. Lev 8 thus serves as the enactment-narrative corresponding to the earlier prescription. The LXX (Vaticanus) translates vayedabber as Καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος (Kai elalēsen kyrios), faithfully rendering both the narrative conjunction (καί) and the divine speech verb. The congregation assembly (כָּל-הָעֵדָה, kol-ha-edah) at the entrance of the tent of meeting is unparalleled in scale for a priestly ceremony — normally ordination rites involve only the principals. The public assembly is theologically important: it establishes the priesthood before all Israel, not merely before God.

2. **vv.6-9 — Washing and vesting:** The vesting sequence in vv.7-9 follows precisely the order of Ex 28 and Ex 29:5-6: tunic (kuttonet), sash (avnet), robe (me'il), ephod with its decorated band, breastpiece with Urim and Tummim, turban (mitsnefet), and golden plate (tsits/nezer). The term נֵזֶר (nezer, "diadem/consecration") at v.9 is significant — nezer is the same word used for the Nazirite's consecration (Num 6) and for a royal crown; its use for the priestly golden plate situates the high priest's consecration in the semantic field of royal-sacred identity. The LXX renders nezer ha-qodesh as τὸ πέταλον τὸ χρυσοῦν τὸ ἅγιον (to petalon to chrysoun to hagion, "the golden holy plate/leaf"), using πέταλον (petalon, "leaf/plate") — a term that emphasizes the thin-beaten metal form of the object. The Leningrad Codex text of v.8 is precise: וַיִּתֵּן אֶל-הַחֹשֶׁן אֶת הָאוּרִים וְאֶת הַתֻּמִּים (vayittten el-ha-choshen et ha-urim ve-et ha-tummim) — Urim and Tummim are placed "into" (el, directional) the breastpiece, not merely "on" it, suggesting they were placed in a pocket or fold of the breastpiece (the "fold" of the choshen is mentioned in Ex 28:16).

3. **vv.10-12 — Anointing sequence: tabernacle before Aaron:** The anointing order is notable: first the tabernacle and all its contents (v.10), then the altar with seven sprinklings (v.11), then Aharon's head (v.12). The sons of Aharon are not individually anointed with oil but only with blood (v.24) and the mixed oil-and-blood sprinkling of v.30. Only the high priest receives the pure anointing oil poured (יִצֹּק, yitsoq, "to pour out/over") on his head — a gesture that in the ancient Near East signifies royal investiture as much as priestly appointment. The verb יִצֹּק (from yatsaq, "to pour") differs from the verb used for the tabernacle and altar anointing (מָשַׁח, mashach, "to anoint by rubbing/smearing"), suggesting a more lavish, poured anointing for the high priest distinct from the rubbing anointing applied to objects. The LXX renders yitsoq (v.12) as ἔχεεν (echeen, "he poured out"), preserving the distinction. The seven-fold sprinkling of the altar (v.11) uses the verb הִזָּה (hizzah, "to sprinkle" — hiphil of נָזָה, nazah), the same verb used for the blood sprinkling in atonement rites (Lev 4:6, 17; 16:14, 19).

4. **v.14 — Semikah / laying of hands:** The semikah (סְמִיכָה, "leaning/pressing of the hand") at v.14 involves both Aharon and his sons pressing their hands on the sin-offering bull. In individual offerings, a single offerer lays one hand on the animal; here the plural "they laid their hands" (וַיִּסְמֹךְ אַהֲרֹן וּבָנָיו, vayismokh Aharon u-vanav — note the singular verb with plural subject, perhaps a collective action) signifies the corporate identification of the priestly family with the sin offering. The semikah gesture communicates the transfer of the offerer's person/identity to the animal — the animal dies bearing the sins/status of the one who lays hands on it. Mishnah Menahot 9:8 and later discussions in the Talmud (Yoma 36a) describe semikah as a firm, two-handed pressing with full body weight in the case of communal offerings. The LXX renders vayismokh as καὶ ἐπέθηκεν Ααρων καὶ οἱ υἱοὶ αὐτοῦ τὰς χεῖρας (kai epethēken Aarōn kai hoi hyioi autou tas cheiras, "and Aaron and his sons placed their hands"), using ἐπιτίθημι (epitithēmi, "to place upon"), which preserves the contact but loses the "leaning with weight" nuance of the Hebrew semikah.

5. **v.15 — Purification of the altar / chattat blood procedure:** The sin offering bull's blood is applied to the horns of the altar and poured at its base (compare Lev 4:7, 18, 25, 30) — this is the standard external-altar chattat procedure. The verb וַיְחַטֵּא (vaychatte, "he purified / he de-sinned") in v.15 is the piel of חָטָא (chata'), functioning here in its denominative sense: "to perform the chattat-rite upon" or "to purify by the sin-offering blood." This verb is the technical term for applying sin-offering blood to an object or person for purification purposes — it is distinct from כִּפֶּר (kapper, "to make atonement") which follows immediately in the same verse. The two verbs together — vaychatte (purification rite performed) then le-khapper alav (to make atonement over it) — indicate that the altar requires both ritual cleansing of any accumulated impurity and formal atonement-consecration before it is fit to serve as the altar of ordination. The LXX renders vaychatte as ἐκαθάρισεν (ekatharisen, "he cleansed"), confirming the purification sense.

6. **vv.18-21 — Burnt offering ram — complete incineration:** The olah ram is wholly burned (v.20, v.21 — kol-ha-ayil, "the whole ram") after being cut into its parts, consistent with Lev 1:6-9. The phrase "for a pleasing aroma (re'ach nichoach), a fire offering (isheh) to the LORD" at v.21 is the standard acceptance formula for the olah. Its appearance specifically in the ordination narrative signals that the ordination has been accepted by God: the aroma of the burnt offering ascending to the LORD is the divine reception of Aharon and his sons' inaugural presentation. The washing of the entrails and legs (v.21) before burning, required also in Lev 1:9, removes the internal filth — the offering must be wholly clean before being wholly given to the LORD.

7. **vv.22-29 — Eil ha-millu'im / ram of ordination:** The ordination ram is the most distinctive sacrificial act of the chapter. Three elements distinguish it from the preceding offerings: (a) the blood-on-ear-thumb-toe rite (vv.23-24); (b) the complex wave offering using fat portions together with the bread from the basket (vv.25-28); (c) the specific assignment of the breast to Moshe as his officiating portion (v.29). The blood-on-extremities rite (tenukh ozen — bohen yad — bohen regel, "earlobe — thumb — big toe") at vv.23-24 has a close parallel in the cleansed leper's purification rite in Lev 14:14-18, where the same three points of the body receive blood and oil. The parallelism is theologically resonant: the leper who re-enters the community and the newly ordained priest who enters sacred service both undergo the same bodily consecration — the whole person (hearing, doing, walking) is marked with blood as belonging to the LORD. The right side in each case (yamin, "right") reflects the ancient Near Eastern convention that the right side is the auspicious, strong, oath-bound side. The LXX renders tenukh ozen as λοβὸν τοῦ ὠτός (lobon tou ōtos, "lobe of the ear"), a precise anatomical rendering that influenced later Greek medical vocabulary. The wave offering of v.27 — placing fat portions and bread loaves on the palms of Aharon and his sons and waving them before the LORD — is the formal gesture of "presenting" the ordination offering: the priests' own hands become the instrument of their first priestly act, holding before the LORD the very offering that consecrates them. This gesture is unique to the ordination ceremony; in normal sacrificial procedure, the offerer waves the breast only, not the fat and bread.

8. **v.30 — Mixed oil and blood sprinkling:** The sprinkling at v.30 is unusual in combining the anointing oil with the altar blood — these two substances are normally kept strictly separate in the sacrificial system (the oil consecrates, the blood atones). Their mixing and joint application to Aharon and his sons and their garments at this unique moment of ordination signals a once-for-all dual consecration: the priests are simultaneously anointed (oil) and blood-purified (altar blood). The Leningrad Codex text is precise: מִשֶּׁמֶן הַמִּשְׁחָה וּמִן-הַדָּם (mi-shemen ha-mishchah u-min-ha-dam, "from the anointing oil and from the blood") — both substances are taken from their respective ritual locations (the anointing oil used in vv.10-12 and the altar blood from v.19). The LXX renders the verse faithfully: ἀπὸ τοῦ ἐλαίου τῆς χρίσεως καὶ ἀπὸ τοῦ αἵματος (apo tou elaiou tēs chriseos kai apo tou haimatos, "from the oil of anointing and from the blood").

9. **vv.31-32 — Eating the ordination meal:** The instruction to boil (בַּשְּׁלוּ, bashlu) the ordination ram's flesh and eat it with the basket bread at the tent entrance is the ordination feast. The verb bashlu (from בָּשַׁל, bashal, "to boil/cook by heat") is specified rather than roasting — perhaps because Lev 6:28 requires that sin-offering vessels be washed or broken after use, and boiling (requiring a pot) rather than roasting (open fire) keeps the flesh more controlled and its cooking vessels more easily subject to purity procedures. The residue is to be burned (v.32) — the ordination meal portions may not be taken away, stored, or given to non-priests. The LXX renders bashlu as ἑψήσατε (hepsēsate, "boil/cook in water"), consistent.

10. **vv.33-36 — Seven-day ordination and the charge:** The charge not to leave the tent entrance for seven days (v.33) is the ordination confinement (paralleling the instructions in Ex 29:35-37). The seven-day period involves the daily repetition of the ordination ceremony — Moshe performs the rites each day for seven days, gradually completing (מַלֵּא, male', "to fill") the investiture of the priests. The phrase יְמַלֵּא אֶת-יֶדְכֶם (yemalle et-yedkhem, "he shall fill your hands") in v.33 is the key technical idiom: to "fill the hand" (male' yad) means to invest with authority and office. The Akkadian cognate expression (mullû qāta, "to fill the hand") is attested in neo-Assyrian texts in the context of royal appointments, confirming that the idiom was a standard ancient Near Eastern technical expression for official investiture, not merely a colorful metaphor. The warning "that you may not die" (ve-lo tamuttu, v.35) underlines that unauthorized departure from the ordained confinement period would be treated as culpable violation of the LORD's charge — consistent with the principle that priestly infractions carry capital consequence (Lev 10:1-3; Num 18:7). V.36 closes the narrative with a compliance formula: "And Aaron and his sons did all the things that the LORD commanded through the hand of Moses" — the phrase be-yad Moshe ("through the hand of Moses") establishes Moshe here specifically as the LORD's authorized agent for the priestly legislation, giving the ordination its grounding not in Moshe's personal authority but in the divine commission mediated through him.

### LXX (Codex Vaticanus) Observations — Pages 92–93

The Vaticanus text (pa_0092–0093) for Lev 8 aligns closely with the MT throughout, with several notable features:

- **vv.2-3 — List of items:** The LXX expands slightly at v.2, adding the conjunction "and" (καί) before several items in the list, smoothing the Hebrew asyndetic listing style into Greek connective prose. The MT lists items more abruptly; the LXX regularizes the syntax for Greek readers.

- **v.8 — Urim and Tummim:** The LXX retains the transliteration τὴν δήλωσιν καὶ τὴν ἀλήθειαν (tēn dēlōsin kai tēn alētheian, "the revelation and the truth") — a translation rather than a transliteration, interpreting Urim (from אוֹר, or, "light") as δήλωσις (dēlōsis, "making clear/revealing") and Tummim (from תָּמִים, tamim, "completeness/integrity") as ἀλήθεια (alētheia, "truth"). This LXX rendering gives the Urim and Tummim a decidedly oracular and epistemic character: they are instruments of divine disclosure of truth, not merely lots. The MT is preserved untranslated throughout the EMET version per project conventions.

- **v.12 — Anointing of Aaron:** Vaticanus reads ἔχεεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν Ααρων (echeen ap' autou epi tēn kephalēn Aarōn, "he poured from it upon the head of Aaron"), faithfully rendering the MT's pouring verb (yitsoq). Some later LXX manuscripts (Old Latin tradition) read "anointed" (ἔχρισεν, echrisen), aligning more closely to the verb mashach; Vaticanus preserves the more precise "poured."

- **v.23 — Blood on the ear:** Vaticanus renders tenukh ozen as τοῦ ὠτίου τοῦ δεξιοῦ Ααρων (tou ōtiou tou dexiou Aarōn, "of the right ear of Aaron") — using ὠτίον (ōtion, a diminutive form, "little ear / earlobe"), which captures the anatomical specificity of tenukh ("lobe, tip") better than the simple ὠτός (ōtos, "ear") of other Greek versions.

- **v.29 — Moses's portion:** The LXX at v.29 specifies Μωυσῆ ἐγένετο ἡ μερίς (Mōusē egeneto hē meris, "to Moses became the portion"), rendering the MT's le-Moshe le-manah ("to Moses as a portion") precisely. The detail that Moshe receives the breast of the ordination ram is unique — it is the only instance in the Pentateuch where Moshe is assigned a sacrificial portion, marking his unique priestly-but-not-Aaronic role in the ordination ceremony.

- **v.35 — "That you may not die":** Vaticanus reads ἵνα μὴ ἀποθάνητε (hina mē apothanēte, "in order that you may not die"), using a purpose clause (ἵνα + subjunctive), which matches the MT's ve-lo tamuttu precisely. The consequence-framing ("do this so that you do not die") appears throughout the Holiness material and is a stylistic marker of the gravitas of the instructions.

— EMET
